# ADR 0003 — Move to folder-autogenerated nav while preserving the IA

- Status: accepted
- Date: 2026-07-08
- Deciders: Manny
- Context source: #docs Slack thread, 2026-07-08

## Context

This is the decision in direct tension with the hard constraint. Manny chose to
**move to the template's folder-autogenerated topics** and, in the same ask,
required that the migration **not change the current docs' content or
information architecture** — same pages, same nav labels/order, same URLs.

Today the docs nav is frontmatter-driven: `scripts/generate-manifest.ts`'s
`buildSidebar()` walks `src/content/docs/docs/`, reads each page's `slug` and
`sidebar.{order,hidden,label}`, and writes `src/lib/generated/sidebar.json`,
which `astro.config.mjs` imports as Starlight's `sidebar`. The template instead
uses Starlight's native `autogenerate: { directory }` wrapped in
`starlight-sidebar-topics`.

Switching mechanics risks changing exactly the nav/labels/URLs we must keep
identical. This ADR records why the switch is safe and where the one real risk
sits.

## Decision

Adopt Starlight's **folder-autogenerated** nav (via `starlight-sidebar-topics`),
and retire the custom `generate:manifest` sidebar pipeline — **while
reproducing the current nav and URL set with zero user-visible diff.**

## Why the IA survives the switch

The current URLs and nav placement already derive from the same signals
Starlight's native autogeneration reads, so most of the IA transfers for free:

1. **URLs.** Docs already live at `src/content/docs/docs/<section>/<page>.mdx`
   with `slug: docs/<section>/<page>` mirroring the file path (verified across
   all 59 pages). Starlight `autogenerate: { directory: 'docs' }` derives
   `/docs/...` routes from the same tree, producing identical URLs. The
   nav-mechanic switch needs **no slug changes and therefore no new redirects.**
2. **Order, visibility, per-page labels.** These come from `sidebar.order`,
   `sidebar.hidden`, and `sidebar.label` frontmatter. Starlight's built-in
   autogeneration reads those three fields natively — our custom generator reads
   nothing extra. Ordering and per-page overrides transfer unchanged.

## The one real risk (watch item for Phase 3)

**Section/group labels.** Our generator derives a group's label from its index
page's `sidebar.label ?? title`, giving human labels like *"Get the most out"*
and *"Work the queue"*. Starlight's raw folder autogeneration instead
**title-cases the folder segment**, which would render *"Get The Most Out"* and
*"Work The Queue"* — a visible IA diff.

Mitigation: set each group's `label` **explicitly** in the
`starlight-sidebar-topics` config in `astro.config.mjs` (the template's config
already labels its groups this way), reproducing the exact current labels. The
current label set to reproduce, in order:

`Start Here` · `Connect` · `Tune` · `Work the Queue` · `Get the Most Out` ·
`Scale` · `Audit` · `Security` · `Reference`

Phase 3's acceptance test is a byte-level diff of the rendered nav against
`main`; if any label/order/grouping cannot be reproduced, that is a
stop-and-escalate, not a silent IA change.

## Consequences

- `buildSidebar()` and the imported `sidebar.json` are removed; group labels now
  live in `astro.config.mjs` config, not derived from index-page frontmatter.
- **`route-manifest.json` generation stays.** `generate:manifest` also produces
  `route-manifest.json`, which the `[...slug].md.ts` endpoint uses to serve
  Markdown variants. Only the sidebar half of the script is retired; the
  route-manifest half remains.
- `redirects.json` is untouched by this switch (still hand-maintained; no new
  redirects needed) and continues to be imported by `astro.config.mjs`.
- **Knowledge-base impact:** the "sidebar.json is frontmatter-generated via
  `generate:manifest`; do not hand-edit it" guidance in shared `PROMPTLESS.md`
  and this repo's `AGENTS.md` becomes false when this phase lands and must be
  rewritten to describe folder-autogenerated topics + config-level labels.
  Flagged to dispatch (LTC edit is dispatch-owned); it should follow the switch.
